latest.title: Covenant Waiver Extension
latest.content:
Today 07:00
RNS Number : 8208U
Litigation Capital Management Ltd
02 March 2026
2 March 2026
Litigation Capital Management Limited
("
LCM
" or the "
Company
")
Covenant Waiver Extension
Litigation Capital Management Limited ("LCM"), an alternative asset manager specialising in dispute financing solutions internationally, announces that the debt covenant waiver from Northleaf that was due to expire at the end of February 2026 has been extended to 15 April 2026. During the extended waiver period the interest on the loan remains increased by 2.00% per annum consistent with the initial waiver. There is no additional one-time waiver fee in relation to this extension.
The Strategic Review that was first announced on 15 September 2025 continues to progress and this waiver extension is reflective of Northleaf's ongoing support while LCM works towards a long-term resolution of its capital position. LCM management will provide an update on the progress of the Strategic Review in due course.
Enquiries

Increase in Credit Facility and Covenant Waiver Extension
Litigation Capital Management Limited ("LCM"), an alternative asset manager specialising in dispute financing solutions internationally, announces that it has to increase its credit facility with funds managed by Northleaf Capital Partners (Canada) Ltd ("Northleaf") from US$75 million to US$100 million. Furthermore, the debt covenant waiver from Northleaf that was due to expire at the end of January 2026 has been extended to 1 March 2026. During the extended waiver period the interest on the loan remains increased by 2.00% per annum consistent with the initial waiver. There is no additional one-time waiver fee in relation to this extension.
The Strategic Review that was first announced on 15 September 2025 continues to progress and this waiver extension is reflective of Northleaf's ongoing support while LCM works towards a long-term resolution of its capital position. LCM management will provide an update on the progress of the Strategic Review in due course.

latest.title: Positive development on case
latest.content:
Today 14:07
RNS Number : 5649O
Litigation Capital Management Ltd
12 January 2026
12 January 2026
Litigation Capital Management Limited
("
LCM
" or the "
Company
")
Positive development on international arbitration claim
Litigation Capital Management Limited ("LCM"), an alternative asset manager specialising in dispute financing solutions internationally, announces a positive development in the international arbitration claim against the Republic of Poland (Poland) that it funded. As previously announced on 8 October 2024, the funded party achieved a successful outcome, securing two separate awards - one under the Energy Charter Treaty (ECT) of £252 million plus interest; and one under the Australia-Poland Bilateral Investment Treaty (BIT) of £183 million plus interest. There is no double compensation meaning that total damages cannot exceed £252 million plus interest.
Poland commenced set-aside proceedings in Singapore (ECT) and London (BIT) to attempt to annul the original awards. The Singapore proceedings recently concluded and the Singapore court rejected in its entirety Poland's application to set-aside the ECT award. Poland has a right to an appeal and such an appeal must be filed by 6 February 2026. We anticipate an appeal will be filed and expect the Singapore court to resolve such an appeal in the second half of 2026.
LCM has invested into this case alongside Fund I investors with the total investment being US$11.3m with US$2.8 million coming from LCM's own balance sheet and US$8.5 million from Fund I. LCM's returns are calculated as a rising multiple of invested capital over time plus interest. LCM's contractual entitlement on this case currently stands at approximately US$71.9 million with US$18.0 million of that flowing to LCM shareholders and US$53.9 million flowing to Fund I investors. LCM does not anticipate earning a performance fee from Fund I investors on this case as a result of previously announced adverse case results impacting Fund I.
Enquiries

latest.title: Adverse Costs Order in Class Action Investment
latest.content:
24 Dec 2025 07:00
RNS Number : 8063M
Litigation Capital Management Ltd
24 December 2025
24 December 2025
Litigation Capital Management Limited
("
LCM
" or the "
Company
")
Adverse Costs in Class Action Investment
Litigation Capital Management Limited (AIM:LIT), an alternative asset manager specialising in dispute financing solutions internationally, announces that the Federal Court of Australia has quantified the costs in the class action funded by LCM on behalf of the Queensland users of electricity against Stanwell Corporation Ltd and CS Energy Ltd ("QLD Electricity Claim").
On 4 December 2024, LCM announced that the claimant had been unsuccessful in the QLD Electricity Claim (the "Judgment"). The Judgment also required the claimant to meet the costs of Stanwell Corporation Ltd and CS Energy Ltd incurred in defending the QLD Electricity Claim. These costs have now been quantified as follows:
1. A$16.2 million payable to Stanwell Corporation Ltd; and
2. A$16.2 million payable to CS Energy Ltd.
LCM has a policy of ATE insurance available to cover adverse costs for this case. After full use of this insurance policy there is an uninsured component of A$19.9 million which exposes LCM to a payment of A$12.9 million with the remainder to be paid by Fund 1 investors.
As announced on 28 February 2025, an appeal has been filed against the Judgment. That appeal has been set down for hearing for two weeks commencing on 2 March 2026. LCM is also giving consideration to the claimant bringing a separate appeal against the quantification of these costs.
LCM continues to be in proactive conversations with its principal lender. The Strategic Review that was first announced on 15 September 2025 remains ongoing and management will provide an update on its progress in the new year.
Patrick Moloney, CEO of LCM commented
:
"This is a very expensive and complicated piece of litigation which has resulted in large cost orders in favour of the respondents. This quantification was of ordinary and not indemnity costs orders and so are not a reflection of the conduct of the claimant or LCM or of the strength of the claim."
Enquiries
Litigation Capital Management
Patrick Moloney, Chief Executive Officer
David Collins, Chief Financial Officer
Cavendish (Nomad and Broker)
Tel: 020 7220 0500
Jonny Franklin-Adams and Isaac Hooper (Corporate Finance)

company_30s.what_they_do: LIT.L is a litigation‑financing specialist that provides plaintiffs with the capital needed to pursue civil claims. It pays legal fees, court costs and other expenses in exchange for a share of any judgment or settlement.
company_30s.why_they_matter: The legal market is huge – billions of dollars of civil disputes arise each year. Litigation finance lets claimants pursue cases they otherwise couldn’t afford, and it offers investors a new asset class with high potential returns. LIT.L competes with a handful of UK‑based firms and has carved out a niche in high‑value commercial disputes.
company_30s.current_state: The company is juggling several large claims, has recently secured a positive development in one of its biggest cases, and has extended its credit facility to keep operations running. Cash burn is modest but it still faces covenant pressure. The share is trading at a very small fraction of its 52‑week high, reflecting the deep discount investors are applying.

rns_narrative: Legal Momentum: The firm has received a favorable update on a $72 M GRX claim and has secured a favourable judgment in a commercial litigation case. These developments suggest a potential payout that could lift the stock.
Financial Stabilisation: A covenant waiver has been granted and a new credit line extended, giving the company breathing room to service debt and fund its litigation pipeline.
Governance & Outlook: AGM results, a strategic review, and an investor presentation signal that the board is actively managing the business and communicating its long‑term strategy to shareholders.

crowd_tape.bull_narrative: Bulls point to the positive GRX claim update and the possibility of a large payout, arguing that the market has undervalued the company’s exposure to that case.
crowd_tape.bear_narrative: Bears warn that the firm’s debt load and tight credit terms could become a problem if the court ruling is not favourable, and they note the company’s ongoing covenant constraints.
crowd_tape.sentiment_shift: The conversation was neutral before the Jan 12 RNS, but the positive case update has nudged sentiment toward cautious optimism.

whats_happening_now.attention_check: Google Trends for the ticker is flat – the market is essentially silent, which is a good sign for a contrarian play.

near_term_catalysts: A court decision on the GRX claim – a favourable ruling could trigger a price spike.
Announcement of the covenant waiver extension – confirming continued liquidity.
Potential distribution of the $72 M payout to the firm’s balance sheet.
Any new win in the company’s litigation pipeline that lifts its earnings profile.
expect_next.watch_for: Look for a sudden volume spike (at least 2× the 20‑day average) accompanied by a positive news headline, or a formal court judgment confirming a payout.
expect_next.timing_regime: The stock is still in the early phase of a deep‑crash recovery. The APEX score indicates moderate compression, so the next move will likely come as price touches a key support near the 52‑week low before a breakout to the 52‑week high.

buy_vs_sell.bull_case: The share is down more than 70 % from its peak and the market is largely silent, so surprise is likely. If the price climbs to the midpoint of the 52‑week range – roughly three times the current level – the upside is around 200‑250 %. The downside is limited to the current price, which is already a small fraction of the 52‑week high.
buy_vs_sell.bear_case: If the court ruling is negative or the company breaches its debt covenant, liquidity could dry up and the share could fall back to zero. The worst‑case loss is 100 % of the current price.
buy_vs_sell.risk_reward: Upside potential (≈ 250 %) versus downside (≈ 100 %) gives a risk‑reward ratio of roughly 2.5 : 1. The asymmetry is favourable for a contrarian approach.

playbook.if_bullish: Enter on a volume spike that follows a positive court decision or fund distribution. Set a stop about 20 % below the entry to protect against a quick retracement. Allocate no more than 5 % of the portfolio to the position, given the volatility.
playbook.if_watching: Hold cash until you see a clear signal – a formal judgment, a significant payout announcement, or a sustained rise in trading volume. Watch the credit facility renewal dates closely.
playbook.if_passing: Not applicable – there are no imminent delisting or bankruptcy risks identified.

ai_insights.bottom_line: Deep crash, silent market, and a looming litigation payout make LIT.L a contrarian play with high upside and limited downside.
